notable_deed.headline: District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District for 17 uncontested years — from Brunswick army family to Texas bench
notable_deed.story: Hans Teichmueller was born on March 7, 1837 in Brunswick, Germany, to an army officer and a mother from the noble von Girsewald family — whose members included two generals. His father's death left the family in financial difficulty; in 1856, at nineteen, Hans emigrated to Texas. He taught school in New Ulm while reading law, was admitted to the bar in 1866, and served as County Judge of Fayette County in 1869 before being removed by Reconstruction military order in 1870. In 1884 he was elected District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District. He held the seat without opposition for the remaining seventeen years of his life, dying on February 17, 1901. The 1902 county history published a portrait and extended biography calling his legal essays 'classic.' His daughter Mary married Edward Mattingly; their son Leroy carried Hans's family name as his middle name — Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly.

notable_deed.headline: WPA muralist; painted 'The Law, Texas Rangers' for the Smithville post office in 1940 — lived to 99
notable_deed.story: Minette Teichmueller was born in La Grange, Texas on January 27, 1871 — daughter of District Judge Hans Teichmueller — and died in Nacogdoches on August 28, 1970, ninety-nine years old. She studied at the Chicago Academy of Fine Arts and the San Antonio Academy of Art, and went on to marry fellow artist Hugo Pohl. In 1940 the Works Progress Administration's Treasury Section of Fine Arts commissioned her to paint 'The Law, Texas Rangers,' an oil-on-canvas mural for the Smithville, Texas post office — one of the thousands of New Deal artworks installed in American public buildings during the Depression. The family remembers her as 'really avant-garde for her time,' someone who as a teenager slipped away from home to hear Mark Twain read aloud — a scandalous act in a devout Southern household. She outlived her famous father by sixty-nine years.

people.enriched_context: Hugo D. Pohl (1878–1960) — San Antonio painter. His most famous work was the 36×75-foot, 5,600-pound asbestos stage curtain at the San Antonio Municipal Auditorium (1926), depicting the 1718 founding of San Antonio with portraits of Bowie, Crockett, Travis, and Bonham. Curtain destroyed in 1979 fire. Also taught at the Hungarian Academy of Arts (Pál Fried was a student). Reference: Dictionary of Texas Artists, 1800–1945 (Grauer & Grauer); Art for History's Sake (Steinfeldt / Witte Museum).

notable_deed.headline: Painted a 36x75-foot stage curtain for San Antonio Municipal Auditorium — destroyed in the 1979 fire
notable_deed.story: Hugo Pohl painted on a scale that few artists ever attempt. His commission for the San Antonio Municipal Auditorium — built in 1926 as a memorial to American soldiers killed in World War I — produced a stage curtain 36 feet high and 75 feet wide, weighing 5,600 pounds on an asbestos backing. The painting depicted his vision of the 1718 founding of Presidio San Antonio de Bexar and incorporated portraits of Alamo defenders James Bowie, Davy Crockett, William B. Travis, and James Butler Bonham. For more than fifty years it hung in the city's largest public hall. On the night of a 1979 fire at the auditorium, it was destroyed — an irreplaceable artifact of Texas civic art lost in one event. Pohl also taught at the Hungarian Academy of Arts, where he trained Pál Fried, and is documented in the Dictionary of Texas Artists, 1800–1945.

notable_deed.headline: Bought Reeves County mineral rights in April 1901 — three months after Spindletop — whose royalties the family still receives
notable_deed.story: In April 1901, Pearl Johnson Baity purchased land in Reeves County, Texas — raw West Texas range, then home to fewer than 2,000 souls. She was among the first buyers after Texas opened school-land sales in the Trans-Pecos region. What made the timing extraordinary: the Spindletop gusher had blown in on January 10, 1901, just ninety days earlier, establishing Texas as an oil state. Pearl retained the mineral rights when she later sold the surface. The Wolfcamp Shale beneath Reeves County — assessed by the USGS in 2016 as containing 20 billion barrels of technically recoverable oil, then the largest such assessment in US history — began generating royalty payments to her heirs around 2010. Three years after buying that land, Pearl attended the 1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition in St. Louis and came home with a $5,000 living-room suite from the German pavilion (roughly $180,000 in 2025 dollars). She died around 1971, approximately 97 years old.

notable_deed.headline: Builder credited with constructing a substantial portion of early twentieth-century San Antonio
notable_deed.story: William Alexander Baity came from Yadkin County, North Carolina to San Antonio in the late nineteenth century and built a contracting business that left a physical imprint on the city. Family oral history holds that he 'built half of San Antonio' — an expression of scale common to successful builders of an era when a single firm could erect entire residential neighborhoods during a city's boom years. He died in 1945 in the house at 211 Castillo Avenue, San Antonio — the home he built around 1905 in the Lavaca neighborhood just south of downtown, in the same corridor as the King William Historic District where prosperous tradespeople of his generation settled. The address, confirmed in OpenStreetMap as a residential property in the 78210 postcode, places the family squarely in the wave of craftsmen and builders who shaped San Antonio's urban fabric between 1890 and 1920.

Claude was 35, a pediatrician practicing in Austin for six years, trained at UT Medical Branch Galveston, and had served in the Marine Corps in World War I. His body was returned to La Grange, where his father Ed Mattingly was still living. The family's 'we don't talk about him' silence and Shari's recollection of 'probably overdose' are confirmed — though it was specifically a double suicide by narcotic injection with a married woman, openly covered by the press at the time.

notable_deed.headline: Prominent Austin pediatrician; death at the Texan Hotel, January 31, 1934 — covered by AP wire across Texas
notable_deed.story: Dr. Claude Mattingly was thirty-five years old when he was found dead in his room at the Texan Hotel, Austin, on February 1, 1934. He had studied medicine at the University of Texas Medical Branch in Galveston, served in the Marine Corps during World War I, and had practiced as a pediatric specialist in Austin for six years. The bodies of both Dr. Mattingly and Mrs. Rhea B. Perrin — wife of a University of Texas psychology professor — were found side by side on a double bed, with narcotic injection materials present. Justice of the Peace Tom R. Johnson returned a verdict of double suicide. The story ran on the front page of the Brownsville Herald on February 1, 1934, and was carried by the Associated Press and International News Service across Texas through February 6. Claude's body was returned to his boyhood home of La Grange for burial. His father Ed Mattingly was still living there. The family has not spoken of him since.

notable_deed.headline: President of Frost Bank from 1926 — San Antonio institution founded 1868
notable_deed.story: Joseph Hardin Frost became president of Frost Bank — formally the Frost National Bank — in 1926. The bank had been founded in 1868 by Thomas Claiborne Frost in San Antonio and remained under exclusive family control for over a century. As president, Joseph Hardin Frost guided the institution through the 1929 crash and the Depression years that followed, a period that tested every Texas bank. Frost Bank survived when many others did not. Family oral history identifies a 'Frost Bank uncle' — likely Joseph Hardin Frost — as a relative through the San Antonio branches of the family. Whether by blood or marriage, the connection places the Mattingly family in the inner circle of San Antonio's financial establishment during the city's formative decades.

notable_deed.headline: Lived to approximately 107 — one of perhaps five documented American centenarians of his era
notable_deed.story: Family oral history preserves the memory of a Mattingly great-great-great-grandfather born around 1828 who lived to approximately 1935, reaching age 107. At that time, documented centenarians in the United States could be counted on one hand; a man who remembered the years before the Civil War and lived to witness the New Deal would have been a significant figure in any Texas community. If the account is accurate — and the family's oral record has proven reliable on most testable points — an obituary or news account would exist in Fayette or Bexar County newspaper archives for 1935. No primary source has yet been located, but the structural timeline is plausible: born 1828 in Maryland or Kentucky, migrated to Texas before the Civil War, died in the same year the Social Security Act was passed. Longevity runs conspicuously in this line.

notable_deed.headline: Co-patentee of 'Mattingly's Hope' — co-founder of the Maryland Catholic line
notable_deed.story: Cezar Mattingly crossed from England to Maryland with his brother Thomas II, and on September 4, 1666, his name was inscribed alongside Thomas's on the patent for 'Mattingly's Hope' in Charles County, Maryland. While Thomas II eventually settled primarily in St. Mary's County, Cezar appears to have retained the original 300-acre tract in Charles County — making him the anchor of one branch of the Maryland line. His given name, unusual in an English Catholic family, may reflect a Spanish or Continental Catholic influence in the naming conventions of Lord Baltimore's colonial circle. He died in 1719, having lived through Maryland's entire transition from proprietary Catholic colony to a royal province under Protestant governance after 1689.

notable_deed.headline: Secured 'Mattingly's Hope' — 300 acres in Maryland under the patent of September 4, 1666
notable_deed.story: Thomas Mattingly II arrived in Maryland before 1665 with his wife Elizabeth and their children, part of a wave of English Catholics drawn to Lord Baltimore's promised haven. His father Thomas I had died in England before making the crossing. On September 4, 1666, a formal land patent was issued in St. Mary's County for 300 acres — fifty acres for each of six family members who had emigrated — naming Thomas II and his brother Cezar as patentees. The tract was called 'Mattingly's Hope.' Thomas's name appears on the St. Mary's County Rent Rolls from 1663 to 1720, a continuous fifty-seven-year span of documented residence. His will, probated before January 12, 1715, named children including Ignatius, James, and others who would carry the Catholic Maryland line for two more generations before a branch walked west into Kentucky.

notable_deed.headline: Earliest dated bearer of the Mattingley surname — granted 6 virgates and a mill to the Prior of Merton
notable_deed.story: Stephen de Mattingley is the first person in recorded history to carry the surname in a dated document. In 1206, he appeared in the Curia Regis Rolls as 'Stephen de Madingel'' and in the Feet of Fines for Hampshire (Trinity 8 John) when he granted to Walter, Prior of Merton, and his successors in free alms: six virgates of land, thirty acres, several meadows, six shillings and three pence rent, and a mill at Mattingley. He had also received his brother Bartholomew's share of their father Revelendus's estate. The Oxford Dictionary of English Surnames (Reaney and Wilson, 1991) cites this precise transaction as the earliest dated attestation of the Mattingley surname. The mill and the meadows are gone; the name endures.

notable_deed.headline: Last lord of Mattingley to bear the name — sold the manor and ended the family's territorial connection
notable_deed.story: Son of Stephen, Peter de Mattingley was the last of his line to hold the manor that gave his family its name. Around 1236, he sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel, severing the surname's tie to the land permanently. The Victoria County History of Hampshire, drawing on the Feet of Fines and Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii in the British Library, records the transaction plainly: 'Stephen was succeeded by his son Peter de Mattingley, who sold all his property to Geoffrey de Arundel.' After that sale, the name disappears from the village's manorial record for nearly four hundred years. Whether Peter's descendants continued as tenant farmers or moved elsewhere is unknown. A blank of three centuries separates him from the next confirmed Mattingley — a London burial in 1641.

notable_deed.headline: First named lord of Mattingley — recorded in the Pipe Roll of Henry II
notable_deed.story: In 1167, the royal financial accounts of King Henry II — compiled in the Pipe Roll for the thirteenth year of his reign — record Ellis as owner of the village of Mattingley in Hampshire. He is the earliest named person connected to the manor, standing at the head of a line that would carry its place-name as a surname across 850 years and two oceans. The village itself had been called 'Matingelege' since before the Domesday survey of 1086, deriving from 'Matta's ley' — the woodland clearing of a Saxon chieftain named Matta whose people, the Basingas, had settled the Whitewater Valley in the fifth or sixth century. Ellis left no patronym; the surname was still the land itself.

people.context: Gustav Teichmüller (1832–1888) was a documented German philosopher with English and German Wikipedia entries. Brother of Judge Hans Teichmueller of La Grange, Texas. Both brothers abandoned university plans after their father August Teichmüller's death — Hans emigrated to Texas in 1856; Gustav stayed in Europe and became a professor at the University of Basel and the Imperial University of Dorpat (Tartu, Estonia). Ignatius Mattingly (1704-1789), son of Thomas Mattingly II. Will probated April 1789 in St. Mary's County Will Book JJ1, named sons Leonard, Luke, and William. The name Ignatius reflects strong Maryland Catholic Jesuit tradition (St. Ignatius of Loyola). His sons Leonard and Luke were among ~25 Maryland Catholic families who migrated to Nelson County KY in 1785 — the 'Holy Land of Kentucky.' Most likely the 'Ignatius' Shari named at the start of her recording. — Per Agent 28 MD Catholic research: of Leonard Sr's 10 Maryland-born children, daughter Margaret became Sister Margaret Mattingly, founding member of the Sisters of Loretto (1812) — first women's religious order founded west of the Alleghenies.

notable_deed.headline: Patriarch of the Kentucky migration — his sons carried the Catholic Maryland line westward in 1785
notable_deed.story: Born around 1704 in St. Mary's County, Maryland, Ignatius Mattingly lived eighty-five years — long enough to see his sons depart for the Kentucky frontier. His will, probated April 1789 in St. Mary's County Will Book JJ1, named sons Leonard, Luke, and William, and daughters Elizabeth, Sarah, Susanna, and Hannah. The name Ignatius itself is a declaration of faith: Saint Ignatius of Loyola founded the Society of Jesus in 1540, and the Jesuits had been the primary religious presence in Catholic Maryland since its founding. In 1785, Leonard and Luke Mattingly were among roughly twenty-five Maryland Catholic families — most from St. Mary's County — who migrated to Nelson County, Kentucky, establishing what became known as the 'Holy Land of Kentucky.' Ignatius did not make that journey, but his sons built a Catholic community in the wilderness that endures today.

notable_deed.headline: Led the 1785 Catholic Maryland migration to Kentucky; confirmed genetic ancestor of the DNA surname project
notable_deed.story: Leonard Mattingly Jr. was born in 1764 in Leonardtown, St. Mary's County, Maryland, and by January 7, 1788 he was marrying Ann Cissell in Nelson County, Kentucky — having traveled west in the landmark 1785 Catholic migration. That year, roughly twenty-five Maryland families from St. Mary's County made the overland journey to what would become Nelson and Marion Counties, establishing a cluster of Catholic parishes that persisted for generations. Leonard settled in Marion County in the community of Saint Mary, where he died in 1843. He is the earliest confirmed genetic ancestor in the Mattingly-Matney Y-chromosome DNA Surname Project, with multiple independent participants tracing their direct paternal descent to him. The FamilySearch record for his 1788 Kentucky marriage (V5ZD-6SJ) is his surviving documentary signature.

historical_references.shari_claim: Aunt Monette (age 16) snuck out of her family home in Louisiana to see Mark Twain on his lecture tour in the 1880s; Twain was considered not fit company for young people because he was an atheist.
historical_references.portrait_caption: Mark Twain, photograph by A.F. Bradley, New York, c.1907. From Wikipedia/Wikimedia Commons.

historical_references.subject: General Sherman's March
historical_references.shari_claim: Sherman passed through North Carolina burning farms; Pearl Baity's grandfather successfully begged Sherman not to burn their farm, but the devastation around them caused the family to move to Texas after the war.
historical_references.portrait_caption: General William T. Sherman, Union Army commander. Photograph c.1865.

historical_references.subject: Spindletop oil discovery
historical_references.shari_claim: Spindletop spudded in January 1901, shortly after which Shari's great-grandmother bought land in Reeves County, TX, in April 1901.

historical_references.subject: 1903 World's Fair (St. Louis)
historical_references.shari_claim: Great-grandmother attended the 1903 World's Fair (the one on which 'Meet Me in St. Louis' is based) and purchased a duplicate of the Kaiser's living room set for $5,000 from the German pavilion.

historical_references.subject: Domesday Book
historical_references.shari_claim: The Mattingly name appears in the Domesday Book, showing the family was present in England before or at the Norman Conquest of October 1066.

historical_references.subject: Lord Baltimore / Maryland Catholic settlement
historical_references.shari_claim: The Mattingly family came to America in 1660 with Lord Baltimore, settled in Maryland as Catholics fleeing Cromwellian Protestant persecution, and received a land grant called Mattingly's Hope.

historical_references.subject: Oliver Cromwell / English Civil War
historical_references.shari_claim: Cromwell's persecution of Catholics caused the Mattingly family to seek permission from the king (possibly James) to emigrate to America in 1660.
historical_references.portrait_caption: Oliver Cromwell, Lord Protector of England. Miniature portrait by Samuel Cooper, c.1656.

historical_references.subject: Daniel Boone and Boonesborough
historical_references.shari_claim: Mattingly relatives were among about 20 families who followed Daniel Boone from Maryland to Kentucky in the 1780s to found Boonesborough; Mattingly descendants still live there today.
historical_references.correction: Two corrections to the family story: (1) Boonesborough was founded in 1775, not the 1780s. (2) The Maryland Catholic Mattinglys actually migrated to Nelson County / Bardstown KY in 1785 — a SEPARATE stream from Boone's settlement. The Mattingly & Moore Distillery (Bardstown, 1877) and the earlier J. Mattingly 1845 distillery confirm the Kentucky distilling lineage is real.
historical_references.portrait_caption: Daniel Boone, painted by Chester Harding, 1820. National Portrait Gallery, Washington DC.

historical_references.subject: R1b Haplogroup
historical_references.shari_claim: The Mattingly family is R1b Haplogroup, the most common European haplogroup, originating about 15,000 years ago as Indo-European; Shari suspects Basque or Pyrenees-Iberian origin based on high incidence of O negative blood.

historical_references.subject: Basque people and O negative blood type
historical_references.shari_claim: The Basque people have the highest incidence of O negative blood in the world; the second highest is in Ireland and the UK (Celts); Shari hypothesizes a migration from the Iberian Peninsula to the UK about 3,000 years ago accounts for the family's O negative blood.
historical_references.correction: Basques have ~47% Rh-negative overall (world's highest, confirmed) but only ~24-25% O-negative specifically (not ~30% as Shari said). Underlying claim about uniquely high Basque Rh-negativity is correct.

historical_references.subject: Basque language (Euskara)
historical_references.shari_claim: Euskara is a language isolate with no known root; the word for weapon in Euskara is 'stone,' indicating extreme antiquity.

historical_references.subject: Basingas (Wessex tribe, NOT 'Beringa')
historical_references.shari_claim: Shari says the Mattingly surname was created in the 8th century by a chieftain named Mata of the Beringa[?] tribe; she could not find information on the origin of this tribe.
historical_references.correction: The tribe Shari called 'Beringa' was actually the BASINGAS — a documented Wessex Anglo-Saxon tribe led by Basa, whose name survives in Old Basing and Basingstoke (22 km from Mattingley). Matta was a member or chief of a Basingas clan. 'Beringa' is a phonetic corruption of 'Basingas' passed down through generations of oral family tradition.

historical_references.subject: Meet Me in St. Louis (film)
historical_references.shari_claim: Shari says the movie 'Meet Me in St. Louis' was based on the 1903 World's Fair (she refers to it as the 1903 World's Fair, but the film and fair are actually 1904).

historical_references.subject: Mitochondrial Eve / Haplogroup L
historical_references.shari_claim: Shari says the L haplogroup is the oldest lineage in the human species, going back to mitochondrial Eve 240,000 years ago; the L lineage split to M and N and spread globally.
historical_references.correction: Mitochondrial Eve is ~155,000 years old (range 130,000–200,000 BP), NOT 240,000 as Shari recalled. Source: Wikipedia, current consensus.

historical_references.subject: Out of Africa and interbreeding with archaic humans
historical_references.shari_claim: Shari says modern humans left Africa 60,000-80,000 years ago and interbred with Homo erectus, Neanderthals, Denisovans, and unknown 'ghost' populations; this DNA contributes to disease resistance and longevity but also susceptibility to schizophrenia, autism, dementia, and Alzheimer's.

historical_references.subject: Lucy (Australopithecus)
historical_references.shari_claim: Shari references Lucy as resembling a chimpanzee with only a slightly larger brain, contrasting with the subsequent dramatic brain expansion in the human lineage.

historical_references.subject: Wolfcamp Shale
historical_references.shari_claim: Shari describes the Wolfcamp Shale in West Texas as the largest oil field ever found; the family's eight royalty wells are in this formation in Reeves County.

historical_references.subject: Ignatius G. Mattingly (1927–2004)
historical_references.shari_claim: Possibly the 'Ignatius' Shari mentioned at 0:00. American linguist, Yale/Harvard, NSA analyst, professor at UConn, researcher at Haskins Laboratories. The Maryland Catholic Mattingly family commonly used the name Ignatius (after St Ignatius of Loyola).

historical_references.name: Don Mattingly
historical_references.fame: New York Yankees first baseman (1982–1995), 1985 AL MVP, MLB manager
historical_references.relation_to_shari: POSSIBLE distant cousin (Indiana Catholic Mattingly migration corridor; no Y-DNA on record)
historical_references.context: Don Mattingly — born Evansville, Indiana, attended Roman Catholic Reitz Memorial High School. Indiana Catholic Mattinglys trace back through the same KY→IN migration corridor as Hunter's direct line. Father Bill Mattingly. Confidence: POSSIBLE 0.55. No Y-DNA test on record.

historical_references.name: Mack Mattingly
historical_references.fame: U.S. Senator from Georgia 1981–1987 (first Republican popularly elected from Georgia since Reconstruction)
historical_references.context: Mack Mattingly — born Anderson, Indiana. First Republican popularly elected to U.S. Senate from Georgia since Reconstruction. Indiana Catholic Mattingly corridor. Confidence: POSSIBLE 0.40.

historical_references.name: Garrett Mattingly
historical_references.fame: Pulitzer Prize historian — 'The Armada' (1959), Columbia University professor
historical_references.context: Garrett Mattingly — Pulitzer Prize for History 1960 ('The Armada'). Born Washington DC. No documented parental lineage to confirm Mattingly clan branch. Confidence: POSSIBLE 0.30.

historical_references.name: Ken Mattingly
historical_references.fame: Apollo 16 astronaut — orbited the Moon as Command Module Pilot
historical_references.context: Thomas Kenneth 'Ken' Mattingly II — NASA astronaut, Apollo 16 Command Module Pilot (1972), STS-4 + STS-51-C Shuttle commander. Born Chicago. No lineage documentation. Confidence: POSSIBLE 0.30.

historical_references.name: Sir Basil Spence
historical_references.fame: British architect — rebuilt Coventry Cathedral after WWII bombing
historical_references.relation_to_shari: POSSIBLE distant Spence relative (David Spence's surname clan)
historical_references.context: Sir Basil Spence OM OBE — Scottish architect, designed the new Coventry Cathedral (1962) beside the bombed-out medieval ruin. The Spence surname clan is small enough that David Spence (Hunter's paternal grandfather) could be a distant cousin. Confidence: POSSIBLE.

open_questions: IGNATIUS [0:00]: Most likely a Mattingly ancestor named Ignatius (Catholic naming tradition — Maryland Catholic Mattinglys routinely used Ignatian names). A real Ignatius G. Mattingly (1927–2004), Yale/Harvard linguist, exists. Recommend asking Shari directly: 'Who was Ignatius in your family?'

BERINGA TRIBE [13:50, 17:58]: Shari repeatedly names a 'Beringa tribe' as the 8th-century Anglo-Saxon tribe from which chieftain Mata coined the Mattingly name. This may be a mishearing or mistranscription of a known Anglo-Saxon tribal name (e.g., 'Bearinga' is the documented Anglo-Saxon folk name associated with Mattingley, Hampshire). Research needed.

211 CASTILE / CASTILLE SPELLING [4:35, 4:44]: Hunter appears to spell out the street name as C-A-S-T-I-L-L-E but the transcript is garbled ('T I L L E'). The exact spelling of the San Antonio street needs verification against city records.

GRANDMOTHER'S BIRTH YEAR CONTRADICTION [21:30]: Shari says 'My grandmother was born in nineteen o two. 1900. 1900.' — she appears to correct herself twice, leaving her grandmother's birth year as either 1900 or 1902. Needs resolution.

KING 'JAMES' IN 1660 [18:51]: Shari says the king who gave the Mattinglys permission to emigrate was 'might have been James'; in 1660 the king was Charles II (Restoration), not James. James II reigned 1685-88. Possible misremembering.

AUNT MONETTE'S RELATIONSHIP [0:00]: Shari calls Monette 'my aunt Monette, my grandfather's mother' — these two descriptions are contradictory (an aunt vs. a mother). It is unclear whether Monette was Shari's grandfather's mother (great-grandmother) or his aunt (great-great-aunt).

GIGI IDENTITY CONFUSION [1:27, 8:31, 20:42]: Shari uses 'Gigi' and 'great grandmother' interchangeably in several passages; at 20:42 she says 'my grandfather's mother' died at childbirth giving birth to Aunt Mamie, yet elsewhere Gigi (great-grandmother) lived until 1971. These may be two different women; clarification needed.
UNCLE ZAY NAME SPELLING [12:32]: 'Uncle Zay' is likely a nickname or phonetic rendering; the actual given name or surname is unknown. Possible variants: Zay, Zy, Eze, or an abbreviated name.

FROST BANK PRESIDENT'S NAME [12:32]: Shari's mother's brother was president of Frost Bank but is not named in the transcript. Research via Frost Bank historical records or San Antonio Bar Association should identify him.

GREAT-GRANDMOTHER'S BIRTH YEAR [8:31]: Shari says 'born in the eighteen seventies' for her great-grandmother who died in 1971; she was then approximately 93-101 years old, which is plausible but the specific birth year is unknown.

GRANDFATHER'S GRANDFATHER BIRTH YEAR [20:42]: Shari says 'born in '18 or '20, died in 1935' at age 107 — if he died in 1935 at 107, he was born circa 1828, not 1818 or 1820. The birth year estimate needs arithmetic checking.

MATTINGLY ROW / VILLAGE LOCATION [17:35]: Shari says there is 'a little village called Mattingly right outside of Cambridge' and 'the street that goes into Cambridge is called Mattingly Row.' The known Mattingley village is in Hampshire, not near Cambridge. This geographic detail may be incorrect or garbled.

GREAT-AUNT'S FAMILY AND TEXAS GOVERNOR [15:00]: Shari mentions a great-aunt's family member who 'had a farm with the family who became the governor of Texas' — the governor's name is not given; research needed to identify which Texas governor this refers to.
DAVID'S IDENTITY [0:00]: David accompanied Shari at Texas A&M but his relationship to Shari is not specified; he could be a son, husband, partner, or friend.

COCONUT OIL CLAIM [46:05]: Shari claims taking one teaspoon of coconut oil daily eliminates brain plaque; this is a folk remedy not supported by current medical evidence — flagged as an unverifiable health claim.

STEPHANIE MENTION [20:42]: Shari starts to say 'He was born in Stephanie' and then corrects herself ('not Stephanie, Charmaine got this picture'); it is unclear who or what 'Stephanie' refers to — possibly a person, a town, or a slip of the tongue.

HANS_5TH: RESOLVED 2026-04-25 — Hans Teichmueller's 5th child confirmed to exist (Lotto 1902 primary source) but unnamed in any accessible record. Most likely died in infancy pre-1870 (Texas had no statewide vital records before 1903). Targeted next steps: 1880 Fayette County TX census M9-1291 ED 57-65 (FamilySearch login), or Briscoe Center 1908 Hans Teichmueller Biographical Sketch.

dna_lineage.summary: Y-chromosome haplogroup R1b-DF27, with terminal SNP Y14084 unique to all documented descendants of Thomas I (established by FTDNA Big-Y testing). R1b-DF27 formed approximately 4,500 years ago (c. 2500 BCE). The branch entered Britain via the Bell Beaker expansion (2450–1800 BCE), which replaced approximately 90% of Neolithic British male lineages.

medieval_to_early_modern.from: Peter de Mattingley (fl. 1236-1249, sold the manor)
medieval_to_early_modern.to: John Mattingly (~1560 Berkshire, d. 17 Dec 1641 London)
medieval_to_early_modern.explanation: After Peter de Mattingley sold all his property ~1236-1249, the Mattingley surname vanishes from Hampshire manorial records for ~390 years. No individual bearing the surname appears in any published primary source for the area between Peter and John Mattingly (~1560). Family likely continued as yeoman farmers in southern England without leaving manorial records. Direct genealogical descent from Peter to John is etymologically/regionally probable but documentarily unproven.
